content.text: *"God saw all that He had made, and it was very good."*{Genesis 1:31}{A set is a Many that allows itself to be thought of as a One.}{Georg Cantor}

A **set** is one of the most fundamental concepts in 
mathematics—so fundamental that most of modern mathematics can be built 
upon it.

content.title: Set
content.content:
A **set** is an unordered collection of distinct objects called 
**elements** or **members**. If $x$ is an element 
of set $A$, we write $x \in A$ (read "$x$ is in $A$" or "$x$ belongs to 
$A$"). If $x$ is not in $A$, we write $x \notin A$.

content.text:
Sets are typically denoted by capital letters ($A$, $B$, $C$, …) and 
their elements by lowercase letters ($a$, $b$, $c$, …).
There are two common ways to describe a set:

**Roster notation** lists the elements 
explicitly within braces:
\[ A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \]

**Set-builder notation** describes 
elements by a property:
\[ A = \setbuild{x \in \Z}{1 \leq x \leq 5} \]
Both notations describe the same set: the integers from 1 to 5.

content.title: Set descriptions
content.problem:
Express each set in both notations:

1. The set of even positive integers less than 10.

2. The set of vowels in the English alphabet.
content.solution:
1. Roster: $\{2, 4, 6, 8\}$. Set-builder: $\setbuild{x \in \Z}{x \text{ is even and } 0 < x < 10}$.

2. Roster: $\{a, e, i, o, u\}$. Set-builder: $\setbuild{x}{x \text{ is a vowel in English}}$.

content.text: Several sets are so common that they have standard symbols:

content.title: The Empty Set
content.content:
The **empty set** $\emptyset$ contains no elements. 
Be careful: $\emptyset \neq \{0\}$ (the latter contains one element, 
namely 0) and $\emptyset \neq \{\emptyset\}$ (the latter contains one 
element, namely the empty set itself).

content.title: Set Equality
content.content:
Two sets $A$ and $B$ are **equal**, written 
$A = B$, if and only if they contain exactly the same elements:
\[ A = B \iff (\forall x)(x \in A \liff x \in B) \]

content.text:
Since sets are unordered and contain distinct elements:
\[ \{1, 2, 3\} = \{3, 1, 2\} = \{1, 1, 2, 3, 3\} \]

content.title: Subset
content.content:
Set $A$ is a **subset** of set $B$, written $A \subseteq B$, 
if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$:
\[ A \subseteq B \iff (\forall x)(x \in A \limplies x \in B) \]

If $A \subseteq B$ but $A \neq B$, we say $A$ is a **proper subset** 
of $B$ and write $A \subset B$.

content.title: Subsets
content.problem:
Let $A = \{1, 2\}$, $B = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $C = \{1, 2\}$.

- $A \subseteq B$ (every element of $A$ is in $B$)

- $A \subset B$ ($A$ is a proper subset; $3 \in B$ but $3 \notin A$)

- $A \subseteq C$ and $C \subseteq A$, so $A = C$

- $\emptyset \subseteq A$ (the empty set is a subset of every set)

content.title: Power Set
content.content:
The **power set** of a set $A$, denoted $\powerset(A)$, 
is the set of all subsets of $A$:
\[ \powerset(A) = \setbuild{S}{S \subseteq A} \]

content.title: Size of Power Set
content.content: If $A$ has $n$ elements, then $\powerset(A)$ has $2^n$ elements.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Each element of $A$ is either in a subset or not—two choices. With $n$ 
elements, there are $2^n$ possible combinations of choices, hence $2^n$ 
subsets.

marginNotes.text:
For God is not a God of disorder 
but of peace.

By faith we understand that the universe 
was formed at God's command, so that what is seen was not made out of 
what was visible.
